title: AcyclicIffTopologicalOrder

tex_statement.html: A CDMG $G=(J,V,E,L)$ is acyclic if and only if it has a topological order.

tex_proof.raw:
% Claim proof file for row claim_3_2 -- "AcyclicIffTopologicalOrder".
% Auto-generated stub by scaffold/solving_current_row.py at row start. A
% manager agent dedicated to writing the TeX proof fills in the body below.
% The proof must:
%   - Stay close to the lecture notes' proof if one exists (always search
%     the LN first for a `\begin{proof}` block immediately following the
%     claim; copy / lightly edit when present).
%   - Otherwise use the LN paradigm and cite prior claims by their `ref`.
%   - Be self-contained enough that a mathematician can follow it without
%     re-reading the LN.
%   - Have no `sorry`, `omitted`, or "obvious" shortcuts.
%
% Compiles standalone via the `subfiles` package.

\documentclass[main]{subfiles}

\begin{document}
% ref: claim_3_2 (proof, with statement restated for readability)
% title: AcyclicIffTopologicalOrder
\def\rowref{claim\_3\_2}
\phantomsection\label{claim_3_2}

% --- statement (restated) ---------------------------------------------------
\begin{Lem}[AcyclicIffTopologicalOrder]
        A CDMG  $G=(J,V,E,L)$ is acyclic if and only if it has a topological order.
\end{Lem}

% --- proof ------------------------------------------------------------------
% Proof lifted verbatim from lecture-notes/lecture_notes/graphs.tex, lines 228-244
% (the \Claude{...} block immediately following the Lem); the outer \Claude{%
% ... }%end Claude wrapper is stripped.
\begin{proof}
($\Leftarrow$)
Suppose $<$ is a topological order of $G$, i.e.\ $v \in \Pa^G(w) \implies v < w$.
If there were a non-trivial directed walk $v = v_0 \tuh v_1 \tuh \cdots \tuh v_n = v$ ($n \ge 1$),
then $v_0 < v_1 < \cdots < v_n = v_0$, a contradiction.

\medskip\noindent($\Rightarrow$)
Suppose $G$ is acyclic. We construct a topological order by repeatedly selecting a node with no parents in the remaining graph.
Define a sequence $v_1, \dots, v_K$ ($K = |J \cup V|$) inductively: given $v_1,\dots,v_{i-1}$, let $R_i := (J \cup V) \sm \{v_1,\dots,v_{i-1}\}$
and $G_i$ the subgraph of $G$ induced on $R_i$.
Since $G_i$ is acyclic (as an induced subgraph of an acyclic graph) and finite, it has a node $v_i$ with $\Pa^{G_i}(v_i) = \emptyset$
(otherwise, following parent edges would produce a directed cycle).
Choose any such $v_i$.

This gives a total order $v_1 < v_2 < \cdots < v_K$.
If $v = v_j \in \Pa^G(w = v_i)$, then when $w$ was selected, $v$ was still in $R_i$, so $v$ would have been a parent of $w$ in $G_i$, contradicting $\Pa^{G_i}(v_i) = \emptyset$---unless $v$ was already selected, i.e.\ $j < i$. Hence $v < w$.
\end{proof}

\end{document}
tex_proof.html:
Lemma — AcyclicIffTopologicalOrder

A CDMG $G=(J,V,E,L)$ is acyclic if and only if it has a topological order.

Proof.

($\Leftarrow$) Suppose $<$ is a topological order of $G$, i.e.\ $v \in \Pa^G(w) \implies v < w$. If there were a non-trivial directed walk $v = v_0 \tuh v_1 \tuh \cdots \tuh v_n = v$ ($n \ge 1$), then $v_0 < v_1 < \cdots < v_n = v_0$, a contradiction.

\medskip\noindent($\Rightarrow$) Suppose $G$ is acyclic. We construct a topological order by repeatedly selecting a node with no parents in the remaining graph. Define a sequence $v_1, \dots, v_K$ ($K = |J \cup V|$) inductively: given $v_1,\dots,v_{i-1}$, let $R_i := (J \cup V) \sm \{v_1,\dots,v_{i-1}\}$ and $G_i$ the subgraph of $G$ induced on $R_i$. Since $G_i$ is acyclic (as an induced subgraph of an acyclic graph) and finite, it has a node $v_i$ with $\Pa^{G_i}(v_i) = \emptyset$ (otherwise, following parent edges would produce a directed cycle). Choose any such $v_i$.

This gives a total order $v_1 < v_2 < \cdots < v_K$. If $v = v_j \in \Pa^G(w = v_i)$, then when $w$ was selected, $v$ was still in $R_i$, so $v$ would have been a parent of $w$ in $G_i$, contradicting $\Pa^{G_i}(v_i) = \emptyset$—unless $v$ was already selected, i.e.\ $j < i$. Hence $v < w$.

lean.name: isAcyclic_iff_hasTopologicalOrder
lean.code:
/-- claim_3_2 (`AcyclicIffTopologicalOrder`): a CDMG `G` is acyclic
iff it has a topological order. Mirrors
`lecture-notes/lecture_notes/graphs.tex` Lem at line 224 verbatim,
using `CDMG.IsAcyclic` (def_3_6) on the left and the existential
`CDMG.HasTopologicalOrder` (def_3_8) on the right. The `[Finite α]`
hypothesis is needed for the (⇒) direction's parent-free-node
extraction; the (⇐) direction does not use finiteness. The proof
phase can recover a concrete `Fintype α` instance from `Finite α`
via `Fintype.ofFinite` if the constructive enumeration is needed. -/
theorem isAcyclic_iff_hasTopologicalOrder
    [Finite α] (G : CDMG α) :
    G.IsAcyclic ↔ G.HasTopologicalOrder := by

lean_explanation:
The theorem fixes a CDMG `G : CDMG α` over an ambient vertex type `α : Type*` — the LN's silent "let $G = (J, V, E, L)$ be a CDMG" carries no built-in finiteness, so at def_3_1's level `α` is fully polymorphic. The extra `[Finite α]` instance hypothesis encodes the LN's assumption that the node set $J \cup V$ is finite: without it, the (⇒) direction's iterative parent-free-node extraction (LN lines 227--245) does not terminate. The conclusion is the biconditional `G.IsAcyclic ↔ G.HasTopologicalOrder`, with `IsAcyclic` (def_3_6) on the left expressing "no non-trivial directed walk closes into a cycle" and `HasTopologicalOrder` (def_3_8) on the right expressing the existential `∃ r, IsTopologicalOrder G r` — the LN's prose "$G$ has a topological order".

The one Mathlib idiom worth flagging is the choice of `[Finite α]` rather than `[Fintype α]`. `Finite` is the `Prop`-valued finiteness predicate; `Fintype` is its data-valued sibling, which additionally packages a concrete enumeration `Finset α`. Since the iff itself is `Prop`-valued and never mentions an enumeration, `Finite` is the lighter and Mathlib-linter-preferred form, and Lean's instance synthesis still lets a caller in a `[Fintype α]` context apply the theorem without supplying anything explicit (`Finite.of_fintype` fires automatically). When the proof needs a concrete enumeration internally, it recovers one on the fly via `Fintype.ofFinite`.

The shape is the standard "fix a graph, then state an equivalence of two predicates of it" pattern: a single biconditional with no further hypotheses beyond the finiteness instance. Downstream callers reach for `.mp` (acyclic ⇒ has topological order) or `.mpr` (has topological order ⇒ acyclic) to extract whichever direction they need.
design_choices:
**Existential right-hand side `G.HasTopologicalOrder`, not the relation-level `IsTopologicalOrder G r`.** The LN's Lem reads "$G$ ... has a topological order" — the order is existentially quantified. `HasTopologicalOrder` (defined in `TopologicalOrder.lean`) is exactly the unwrap `∃ r, IsTopologicalOrder G r`, so it lines up verbatim with the LN's "has". An alternative iff `G.IsAcyclic ↔ ∀ r, IsTopologicalOrder G r` would be false (the trivial relation is never a topological order of a non-empty graph), and `G.IsAcyclic ↔ IsTopologicalOrder G r` for a *fixed* `r` would be strictly stronger than the LN's statement, which only asserts existence.

**`[Finite α]` instance hypothesis on the iff.** The LN's own proof of the (⇒) direction (`graphs.tex` lines 227--245) iteratively extracts a parent-free node from the induced subgraph on the still-unselected nodes, and that construction terminates only because $J \cup V$ is finite — the LN says explicitly "since $G_i$ is acyclic and finite, it has a node with no parents". def_3_1's `CDMG` is polymorphic in `α` and carries no built-in finiteness, so the assumption must enter as an extra hypothesis somewhere on the iff. The (⇐) direction does not use it; we still attach the hypothesis to the iff itself rather than splitting into two lemmas (see below).

**`[Finite α]` (Prop-valued) rather than `[Fintype α]` (data-valued).** The iff is `Prop`-valued and never mentions a concrete enumeration. Mathlib's `linter.unusedFintypeInType` flags `[Fintype α]` in exactly this situation and recommends `[Finite α]`. The Prop-valued instance is the weakest finiteness hypothesis the statement needs, silences the linter, and still composes with the full `Fintype` API inside the proof (which can recover an enumeration via `Fintype.ofFinite` from `Mathlib.Data.Fintype.EquivFin` whenever it needs one). Callers writing `G.isAcyclic_iff_hasTopologicalOrder` in a `[Fintype α]` context get the `Finite α` instance for free via `Finite.of_fintype`.

**`[Finite α]` rather than an explicit `(G.J ∪ G.V).Finite`.** The set-level finiteness hypothesis would be strictly weaker — it leaves the ambient carrier `α` potentially infinite, constraining only the node set — and matches the LN's prose "finiteness of $J \cup V$" literally. It would also extend more cleanly to settings where the ambient carrier is uncountable (e.g. ℝ-valued nodes in chapter 4 CBNs) while the chosen CDMG itself happens to have a finite node set. We default to the typeclass form for ergonomics: it carries no extra explicit argument at use sites, composes directly with Mathlib's `Finite` / `Fintype` API, and matches the iSCM chapters' default ambient-type assumption. The implication `Finite α → (G.J ∪ G.V).Finite` is one `Set.toFinite` call away, so callers in the more general regime can downshift locally if they need to.

**Single bundled iff, not two separate lemmas.** Splitting into `isAcyclic_of_hasTopologicalOrder` and `hasTopologicalOrder_of_isAcyclic` was considered, but the LN states the equivalence as one Lem; bundling matches that prose and lets `simp` / `rw` rewrite freely between the two predicates. Callers who only need one direction use `.mp` (acyclic ⇒ has topological order) or `.mpr` (has topological order ⇒ acyclic).

**Name `isAcyclic_iff_hasTopologicalOrder`, dot-projection intended; `α` implicit, `[Finite α]` between, `G` explicit.** Downstream callers write `G.isAcyclic_iff_hasTopologicalOrder.mp ha`, which reads as the LN's prose "$G$ is acyclic iff $G$ has a topological order". This parallels every other claim-of-CDMG theorem in the section (`no_arrowhead_into_input`, `input_edge_target_mem_V`, `input_nodes_not_adjacent` in `JNodeProperties.lean`). The binder order — implicit type, then instance argument, then explicit data — is Mathlib's convention for "fix an object, state a property of it" theorems, and lets Lean's instance synthesis resolve `[Finite α]` automatically at every use site that has either a `Finite` or `Fintype` instance in scope.
